sentences.text: Yeah.
And when the war broke out between Russia and Germany, they started to-- they decided to use the, the Polish people as an army.
So [NON-ENGLISH], who was a famous communist in Poland, she organized a, a, a Polish Army.
So first of all, they freed all the Polish citizens from the camps.
The other army was organized by Anders.
As a matter of fact, this was a Polish general.
Begin, as a matter of fact, went with Anders's Army to Palestine at that time.
And he was-- he got-- he asked legally because Begin was old-time gentleman, that you don't go away from an army, even if you hate that army, or that army was only used for a certain purposes.
He had to have a legal dismissal.
And he got it.
And so those-- many of those people who came out of the camps, since they were familiar with working in forests, came back to Russia on the Volga and the Don where I lived.
And they worked again in the camp in the forests.
So he told me, there are several Polish people working in these forests.
If I will go among them, you will be one of them-- to protect me.
But then he said, in the forest are two jobs.
One is to cut the wood-- the trees, which used to be maybe meant as a compliment, or to work on a horse.
Am I familiar with horses?
I said, of course, I'm-- I had two horses home.
I didn't see-- I, I-- the only horse I saw is in the street.
And but I knew it's a simpler-- it is going to be an easier job.
But then I said, I have a friend who was a Pollock from a farmer that I knew.
He was familiar with horses.
And he had two horses.
We got the job.
And there are a lot of things which happened there, but I don't know if they are very important in the things.
I worked-- I became important for the organization.
They wanted to put me in charge of a group.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Except the Jews-- we had, although, in some places in that state, German girls working-- but German girls not from Germany, but Germans from the German-- it was a German autonomic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] district in Russia.
It was  an autonomic state, Oblast, which was mostly German which had came to Russia during the times of Ivan the Great when they wanted to build, to develop the, the, the country.
And they imported German workers.
And they lived, and they spoke German among themselves.
And when the war broke out, and Russia did it, like, we did here with the Japanese, but not in the same nice way.
They took all the republic through all, all the people from that autonomic-- they called it autonomic republic-- and sent them out to Asia.
There they separated-- the men they sent to the coal mines in [NON-ENGLISH].
This is on the Chinese border.
The women they sent to the forests to work, the wood forest, in other words.
And the old people and the kids remained in Asia.
So we-- for us, worked a group-- several groups of the German girls.
And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I could-- I was without papers ever since I escaped.
But since I worked on a horse-- and nobody has private horses in Russia, nobody.

And if I came to the city to pick up supplies for the workers, I was always with the horse, I don't-- didn't have a problem because nobody was checking papers from people who had-- it was a, a big lifesaver.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then we had-- there was an island on the Volga where it was a lot of wood.

So I had to prepare that island, housing and everything, for about 50 or 80 girls, and moving them and then the ice comes until the, the Volga freezes, we-- and we had to prepare wood for the city, and so on.
I made the mistake that, instead to go by, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by horse, with my horse, I went by train.
And by train, there was a check.
I didn't have papers.
They arrested me.
And they sent me to the transit camp and to-- this was already in '44-- as a deserter.
Of course, I didn't have papers.
They didn't trust my stories.
And--
In Syzran.
This is-- there is a, a transit camp for regular people, and it-- for the prisoners.
It was a prison on the transit camp.
Well, I was interviewed by the-- by the chief criminal there.
And to tell the story, he told me, here you tell the truth.
There with the-- with the commandant, you can say whatever you want to say.
Anyhow, I talk-- I told him the story.
I'm a Polish citizen.
I don't know why they keep me.
This was my defense.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] things didn't look good because they used to send those people, deserters-- and they had special battalions.
They called them [NON-ENGLISH], which is for criminals.
And they used to go on the front.
And the first one without arms to go on the fields to blow up the mines-- and this is out of thousands, maybe 10 remained alive or something like that.
And I was supposed to end up with this end.
Then I met-- I saw people, friends that I knew, Jewish people, that were going to the Polish army.
And I told one to give me a bottle of vodka.
He brings me a bottle of vodka.
Next morning, the, the sergeant comed in-- comes in to check.
I give him the vodka.
He said, why?
I want to go to the commandant of this-- of the camp.
He said, you cannot.
You have to write.
I said, I wrote already a letter.
He doesn't read it.
But the vodka speaks very-- much better than anything else.
He took me to the-- gave me to, to soldiers around with a-- with a gun, with a-- with a rifle in the back and the front, like a real gun.
And they took me to the commandant of the-- of the camp.
And I started to tell them what is my story.
I am a Polish citizen.
I want to go to the Polish army.
Why do they send me to the Russian army?
And I worked all the time for this organization, which was called [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is an abbreviation-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] city, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] city-- the supply of energy to the city.
All the fields, wood, coal, oil came from this organization.
And I said, I-- why don't you check?
And he said, oh, I'm glad-- he tells me, I'm glad you told me.
I need some wood.
So he calls up, and he finds my-- my director knew, the, the one who protected me, that I'm in trouble.
But he couldn't do anything because he had to protect himself.
So when that guy called him up, and they talk, talk, talk.
And then I, I heard the story later on.
By the way, I understand you have one of my people there.
And then came out the conversation.
So he told him that I'm the best worker, and I was going on a-- on a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the special assignment.
And he-- that, that guy, until he doesn't release me, he is going to-- not going to get any wood down at the camp.
OK, he said.
Now you can go.
But now it happens, I think, the night before, in this camp, they brought in people who-- they spoke Ukrainian, and who missed their train going on the front, military soldiers.
And I started to talk, you know, where are you?
One tells me, I'm from Lutzk.
I thought maybe he was next to Lutzk.
To make a long story short, that guy was the super of our house who survived-- I mean, survived, he was a goy.
And he was mobilized after the Russians liberated our town.
And, and [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I can claim-- this I said ahead of time-- since my town was liberated, I didn't want to go back to work there.
I wanted to be sent to the Polish army to come back to home, maybe somebody survived.
In February of 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was maybe a couple of months later.
And since it was-- so I wanted to go to the Polish army.
And I applied for it-- I told this.
Then the same night, I met that Ukrainian that survived from the-- from the train, who-- and he told me about a distraction.
We couldn't-- we didn't believe that things happened, what happened.
Even if they wrote in the papers-- first of all, the Russian paper didn't stress it too much.
And, and we-- in Russia, you don't trust the official news because everything is propaganda.
So we thought maybe they exaggerated.
And that told me, if I would have met him a day before, maybe I would still stay until the end of the war in Russia because my boss told me, now you'll get papers.
And you'll be free until the end of the war.
And from our organization, they didn't take to the army.
This was-- since this was a important industry.
But anyhow, I applied to go through the Polish army.
It took us three months until we arrived to-- the headquarters of the army was in [NON-ENGLISH] By the time we came to [NON-ENGLISH], it was already in Lublin.
Of course, Lublin was--
So we came to Lublin.
And there I met Jews.
And we-- I just walked away.
And, and that's it.
And I became a refugee from the camps-- who came from the camps and-- When did you find out-- or what did you find out about what happened in Lutsk?
That all the Jews were destroyed.

Because when he left, he didn't see survivors because they mobilized him a little while after he came-- later on came maybe like-- I mentioned my partner, I did-- that later on, people who survived came from little villages or from the forest, partisan.
But when he told that it was a total destruction of all the people, not only from Lutsk, but all the Jews from the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] villages and towns around Lutsk.
Because Lutsk was the major town in this state.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Lublin, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met people from my town.
And I met some friends, people from Numir who knew my family.
And after maybe a month later came Fenia's husband, who grew up on the same street where I grew up.
And we knew each other all our lives.
He wanted-- was sent to the Polish army.
And for him-- for me, I didn't have a legal dismissal.
But for him, I knew the, the, the editor of the paper, of the communist paper, was a man from Lutsk, [NON-ENGLISH], that I knew from before the war.
And when he, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] he gave us a contact to get-- to be free from the army legally.
We stayed in Lublin a couple of months.
But I was arrested.
I ran away.
But that's a different story, so it'll-- two little adventures.
And we made fake papers that we are going to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hungary.
And we traveled.
While we were on the train, the war ended in May of-- May or June of '45.
We were in the train.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how much detail you want to hear because there were so many things happening in the-- times were not normal.
And we behaved also not in a normal-- not in a completely rational way.
Because if we would be rational, we wouldn't take those chances.
Anyhow, we left Russia.
We came to Poland.
We go now to Romania.
And we have-- and we are passing again Russia because they occupied part of that.
So we have-- we had the same-- again, the Russians.
It took me thousands miles to go away, hundreds of miles to go away from them.
We ended up in Romania.
It was a camp in [ROMANIAN], the name of the city.
After a few months, a friend of mine came.
And we were talking-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was decided we are going to go-- I mean, he was able to take us from Romania to Austria to the Russian zone.
And then we are going to worry later on.
But the, the funny part is the papers that we made-- we made ourselves the papers-- the same papers from Poland to Romania, we made ourselves.
We had stamps, all kind.
In Russia what is important is a round stamp.
We had round stamps there, so-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and so, so we made papers that-- to go from Hungary to Romania.
But the papers, we went from Romania to Hungary.
Anyhow, we passed the Hungarian border.
And then we came to Austria, to Graz.
This was the major center of the Russian occupation zone in Austria.
Austria had three occupation zones, Russian-- I mean, Russian-- four-- Russian, American, British, and French.
We ended up in that.
The only problem, problem was to go from the Russian zone to any other zone from the not desirable British, but it depends, and from there into Italy.
And then it was hopefully to Israel.
But when we came, the border was closed.
So my friend, that-- which I met in, in [ROMANIAN]-- we took two bottles of vodka.
We took a little suitcase.
We went on the border.
The Russians should arrest us.
And they arrested us.
They started to search us.
They opened-- we opened that little briefcase.
What is there?
They see.
What-- what's this?
I said, don't you know what it is?
You want a drink?
And we started slowly to drink.
After we finished the first bottle, drinking, we could-- we knew very well this is a good score we had.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They asked-- became-- they became very friendly now.
And so, what can you do for us?
I'll tell you something.
Basically, there is something you could do for us.
We are seven people-- the Russian-- we had a story prepared that we-- how did we know Russian?
Because we lived with Russians in the camps, in the, the German camps.
And the Russians helped us all the way.
And the British don't let us go out the rest of the border.
Oh, what kind of bad people the British are.
They're used not, not such a nice language.
You know, come tomorrow morning.
And bring the people.
We'll let you through.
OK.
We come tomorrow morning, seven people, we another five.
They go through.
And my friend and I stayed.
Why don't you go?
So we said, to tell you the truth, we have some more friends who would love to go.
Anyhow, we made a deal-- for every group of 12 people, we give them a bottle of vodka and a watch.
So all of the bad watches from all over Europe ended up in our place.
And we gave them to the Russians because they didn't-- money was no value for them.
Foreign money, they didn't know what it is.
The only currency which was a very valuable for them were the watches.
So-- and this-- but this deal was going on for a while.
And everything was-- until it went that the big powers made an agreement.
They moved the border from Graz to, I think, a different town.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And now we have-- we had a problem with the British.
The British were stopping us all over the place.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the British zone, we already-- it was not a question of secrecy.
But we just couldn't cross the border.
So we went to Budapest in Hungary.
And we created a Red Cross.
We, we printed all kind of forms with stamps.
And we became a Red Cross, sending people for a-- from a British camp in Klagenfurt, which it was, to Albania-- Jews from the camps.
After a couple of months came a letter from the Albanian government.
It didn't have so many Jews before the war, how many we sent?
So we started to send to Italy Jews-- everything to, to be able to go to Italy.
And from there, it's already-- everything was organized in Italy, the-- all the organizations, they had camps, and the cooperation of the Italian government, and everything.
So God works in mysterious ways-- comes a delegation of the Greek Red Cross to visit their quote, 'nationals' in Klagenfurt and for a-- they say it Yiddish, [YIDDISH], for even-- for our medicine.
We didn't have one Greek Jew.
[LAUGHS] Nobody spoke Greek.
So they came.
They talked-- we told them, tell a story, that you were born in Salonika.
Your parents went to Poland.
You were born.
And after, your parents went when--
But of course, it-- and after a few weeks, we're getting a letter from the Austrian authorities that we should present ourselves.
They all-- going in the president.
And we meet the president of the Red Cross, the vice-president, the secretary, everything-- nice titles.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So the first impulse was to run, which, for us, was no problem.
We didn't have borders.
Europe didn't exist for us-- borders.
Any place we could go because we knew how to do it.
But, since we still had a few hundred people and we wanted to liquidated the camp-- so we decided, since I had English as a foreign language in-- in high school-- in gymnasium-- so I will go.
And then we'll see what's going to be.
I come in.
And I go from the German-- from the Russian to the British.
There are sitting two people.
And I had papers at the time, a Jew from Trieste.
I show him my papers.
We made them ourselves.
He said, yes, do you speak Italian?
I said, yes.
He started to speak to me Italian.
I said, well, as a child, I left Trieste.
And my parents [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took me to Poland.
Do you speak other languages?
I started to speak Polish, Russian.
I make everything except-- do you speak Hebrew also? he asked me.
I said, yes.
He started to speak to me in Hebrew.
But we knew that the British had agents who spoke Hebrew, Hebrew.
But there comes a point and-- so then he starts to tell me in Hebrew that he knows exactly what we are doing.
And they are Israeli.
They work for the British intelligence because the British use German Jews to interrogate Germans, the Austrians, the Nazi collaborators.
It comes a point in that playing the cat, cat and mouse that you just say, OK, that's true.
What's it going to be?
Good to know.
Now we'll talk.
We'll give you two weeks.
Liquidate the camp.
Send all the people somewhere.
And we will hold the papers.
Because the papers from the Austrian-- that I didn't mention that the Greek Red Cross delegation wrote a letter that this is not Greek, that something was wrong with the whole set-up.
We'll keep that letter for two weeks.
And you'll liquidate.
And, and that's it.
And we all-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we did exactly what he told us.
But again, things don't happen so-- nothing goes easy.
So we were stopped on the border-- our train.
The two wagons with the 200 people, they were disconnected.
They don't go here.
They don't go here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we started to walk.
Actually, it wasn't so simple.
We said, we're going to take a look at what's going on.
But the people just didn't trust us.
So they were scared that, that we will come back.
So they started to follow us.
And the British stopped us.
Where are you going?
We want to go to Austria.
We didn't say that we come Austria.
We want to go Austria.
But our papers are not correct.
He said, it's no problem.
I'll let you through.
I said, no.
If you let us through, they'll stop us again.
So what do you want?
We want to go back to Treviso, to [INAUDIBLE].
So they gave us trucks.
And they took us to Treviso.
And there, everybody was, was on for his own.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So this was the experience with the Austrians.
This is called the Brihah.
Brihah was an organization which were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taking the Jews from the Russians and also from all over Europe to Italy, to-- mostly to Italy or to Germany, to the western site.
And from there, hopefully, it was not Israel yet-- that this was-- I'm talking this-- everything was in 1946.
How many people do you estimate that you helped bring across?
Oh, a few hundred people, 300, 400, altogether between both systems.
And actually, my idea was to go to study engineering.
This is what I wanted to do always.
So I think, now we go after Hitler, I'm going to study engineering.
But in the meantime, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Israel came a, a delegate.
He happened to be [NON-ENGLISH], my friend from high school, from [NON-ENGLISH].
And he had three names that he knew that they are in Italy.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] well, well, one, I have pictures of them.
But I'll show you the pictures.
And among the third name, was I.
And he started to organize the Irgun.
And after a while, we had military school, training schools.
And when-- before the state, we're already buying guns, shipping guns to Israel.
From Italy and from France.
I, I, I don't know if you're familiar with that ship This was the ship that Ben-Gurion sent.
This was the ship which took a lot of arms from Italy and from France to Israel.
And then but Rabin was shooting-- Ben-Gurion gave the order.
Rabin shot that-- sunk that boat in Israeli harbor.
This is politics again, a different story.
Anyhow, so we started to organize Irgun.
We had training schools.
We were sending out often the courses to organizers to Germany, to Austria, to Romania, to a lot of Europe.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so this was going on until the establishment of the state of Israel.
I met, in the meantime, my wife.
She was Italian citizen.
And she was a doctor, a medical doctor.
She practiced dentistry because in Italy, there is no special school for dentistry.
You get a medical degree and-- because her first husband was a dentist, also.
He was Italian.
Janet.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] since she was a-- she had very good connections in Italy.
After the Gasperi's daughter, daughter-- the Gasperi was the prime minister.
His daughter was a-- we had a connection.
Through her, we arranged all kinds of things.
And through her, we got very good connection with the Italian authorities.
And we could do a lot of things in Italy.
And, and after the establishment of the state, since I had all the connections with the authorities, everybody left-- many, most of them left for Israel.
And I still had to liquidate everything, which was-- you know, we couldn't just pick ourselves.
It was-- people were involved, Italian citizens, Jewish-Italian citizens, lawyers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Also, we arranged all kind of passports we could get for the-- if the Joint Distribution wanted to send people to South America, there were, at that time, Nelson Passports.
I could get them from the government because I had the connections with the Italian ministry, internal ministry, foreign ministry.
So I was supposed to stay a little while longer.
Mm-hmm.
